county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Howard County formed 1873 from Hempstead, Pike, Polk, and Sevier counties. County seat is Nashville (not to be confused with Nashville, Tennessee). Named for James Howard, a state senator.
county_metadata.research_tips: Howard County records at the County Clerk's office in Nashville are complete from 1873. This southwest Arkansas county was a timber and farming area. The county seat of Nashville (not to be confused with Nashville, Tennessee) serves as the county's civic center. The Howard County Historical Society maintains research files and local history materials. Dierks and other lumber company records document families in the timber industry. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. The Southwest Arkansas Regional Archives (SARA) in nearby Washington also holds regional materials that may include Howard County families.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Howard County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Nashville holds deed books from 1873

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Independence County formed 1820 from Lawrence County. County seat is Batesville, one of the oldest settlements in Arkansas. Batesville was a key White River steamboat landing. The county was a parent county for many other Ozark counties.
county_metadata.research_tips: Independence County records at the County Clerk's office in Batesville are well-preserved from 1820. Batesville, one of the oldest settlements in Arkansas, was a key White River town and steamboat landing. The Independence County Historical Society and the Old Independence Regional Museum in Batesville have strong local history collections. Lyon College (formerly Arkansas College) archives hold institutional and community records. This county was a parent county for many Ozark counties, so early families may appear in Independence records before their destination counties were formed. FamilySearch has extensive microfilm of Independence County records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Independence County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Batesville holds deed books from 1820

source_profiles.survival_notes: Deed books complete from 1820; housed at County Clerk's office in Batesville

county_metadata.record_losses: 1866 courthouse fire damaged some early records; deed books partially survived
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Izard County formed 1825 from Independence County. County seat is Melbourne. Named for George Izard, territorial governor. The 1866 courthouse fire destroyed early records. Parent county for several Ozark counties.
county_metadata.research_tips: Izard County's 1866 courthouse fire destroyed early records. The County Clerk's office in Melbourne holds post-fire records. This Ozark county was remote and sparsely populated, with subsistence farming as the primary economy. The county was a parent county for several later-formed Ozark counties, so early settlers may first appear in Izard records. The Izard County Historical Society maintains research files. Small church records and cemetery transcriptions are critical for this area. For pre-1866 records, check Independence County (parent) and the Arkansas History Commission. FamilySearch has microfilmed surviving county records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Izard County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Melbourne holds deed books from 1825

county_metadata.record_losses: 1870s fire damaged early records; many were reconstructed from state copies
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Jackson County formed 1829 from Independence County. County seat is Newport, on the White River. Newport was a significant river town and later a railroad junction. The 1870s fire destroyed early records.
county_metadata.research_tips: Jackson County's 1870s fire destroyed early records, though some were reconstructed from state copies. The County Clerk's office in Newport holds surviving records. Newport, on the White River, was a significant river town and later a railroad junction. The Jackson County Historical Society maintains local research files. The county straddles the transition zone between the Ozark foothills and the delta lowlands, creating diverse settlement patterns. Civil War battles occurred in the county (Battle of Whitney's Lane). Freedmen's Bureau records are available for African American families. FamilySearch has microfilmed surviving county records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Jackson County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Newport holds deed books from 1829

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Jefferson County formed 1829 from Arkansas and Pulaski counties. County seat is Pine Bluff, a major Arkansas River port city. Had one of the largest enslaved populations in Arkansas. Home to the University of Arkansas at Pine Bluff, a historically Black university.
county_metadata.research_tips: Jefferson County records at the County Clerk's office in Pine Bluff are well-preserved from 1829. Pine Bluff was a major Arkansas River port city and cotton center. The county had one of the largest enslaved populations in Arkansas, making estate inventories and probate records essential for African American research. The University of Arkansas at Pine Bluff (a historically Black university) archives hold institutional and community records. The Pine Bluff/Jefferson County Library System has extensive genealogy collections. Freedmen's Bureau records are particularly detailed for Jefferson County. FamilySearch has extensive microfilm. The Jefferson County Historical Society is active in record preservation.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Jefferson County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Pine Bluff holds deed books from 1829

county_metadata.record_losses: 1864 Civil War destruction; courthouse burned; most early records lost
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Johnson County formed 1833 from Pope County. County seat is Clarksville. Located in the Arkansas River valley in the Ozarks. The 1864 Civil War courthouse burning destroyed most early records. Coal mining was important from the 1880s.
county_metadata.research_tips: Johnson County's courthouse was burned during the Civil War in 1864, and most early records were lost. The County Clerk's office in Clarksville holds post-war records. This Ozark county was bitterly divided during the Civil War with both Union and Confederate units organized in the county. Coal mining in the 1880s-1900s brought new families and is documented in mining company records. The Johnson County Historical Society maintains reconstructed records. For pre-war families, check parent county (Pope) records and GLO land patents. The University of the Ozarks in Clarksville has institutional archives. FamilySearch has microfilmed post-war county records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Johnson County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Clarksville holds deed books from 1843

source_profiles.survival_notes: Early deed books damaged or destroyed; 1864 Civil War destruction; courthouse burned; most early records lost. Post-loss records at County Clerk's office are generally complete

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Lafayette County formed 1827 from Hempstead County. County seat is Lewisville. Located in southwest Arkansas on the Red River. Major plantation and slaveholding county in the antebellum period.
county_metadata.research_tips: Lafayette County records are at the County Clerk's office in Lewisville, with good survival from 1827. This southwest Arkansas county on the Red River was a major plantation and slaveholding area. Estate inventories in probate records are detailed and include enslaved persons. Freedmen's Bureau records at NARA are essential for African American research. The county's proximity to the Texas border means some families appear in both states' records. The Southwest Arkansas Regional Archives (SARA) in Washington holds regional materials. FamilySearch has microfilmed Lafayette County deed books and probate records. Check the Arkansas History Commission for early territorial records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Lafayette County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Lewisville holds deed books from 1827

county_metadata.record_losses: 1864 courthouse fire during Civil War destroyed records; county clerk's records partially reconstructed
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Lawrence County formed 1815 from New Madrid (Missouri Territory), one of the earliest counties in the future state. County seat is Walnut Ridge (moved from Powhatan). The 1864 courthouse fire during the Civil War destroyed records. Parent county for many northeast Arkansas counties.
county_metadata.research_tips: Lawrence County's 1864 courthouse fire during the Civil War destroyed records, though the county clerk partially reconstructed records afterward. The County Clerk's office in Walnut Ridge holds surviving and reconstructed records. As one of the earliest counties in the state (1815, formed from Missouri Territory), Lawrence was a parent county for many northeast Arkansas counties. Early families may appear in Lawrence records before destination counties were formed. The Lawrence County Historical Society and the Powhatan Historic State Park in Powhatan (the old county seat) are excellent resources. FamilySearch has microfilmed surviving county records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Lawrence County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Walnut Ridge holds deed books from 1825

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Lee County formed 1873 from Crittenden, Monroe, Phillips, and St. Francis counties. County seat is Marianna. Located in the Arkansas delta. One of the largest cotton-producing counties with a majority African American population.
county_metadata.research_tips: Lee County records at the County Clerk's office in Marianna are complete from 1873. This eastern Arkansas delta county was one of the state's largest cotton-producing areas with a majority African American population. The 1919 Elaine Massacre (a racial violence event documented in national archives) occurred in southern Lee/Phillips County area. Freedmen's Bureau records and plantation records are essential. The Lee County Museum in Marianna has local history materials. Estate inventories and probate records are important for both white and African American family research. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Lee County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Marianna holds deed books from 1873

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Lincoln County formed 1871 from Arkansas, Bradley, Desha, Drew, and Jefferson counties. County seat is Star City. Located in the cotton and timber belt of south-central Arkansas.
county_metadata.research_tips: Lincoln County records at the County Clerk's office in Star City are complete from 1871. This south-central Arkansas county was in the cotton and timber belt. The Star City Public Library holds local history materials. Probate records contain enslaved persons inventories for families predating Lincoln County's formation who appear in parent county records (Arkansas, Bradley, Desha, Drew, Jefferson). The Lincoln County Historical Society maintains research files. Freedmen's Bureau records at NARA cover formerly enslaved families. FamilySearch has microfilmed Lincoln County records. Check parent county records for pre-1871 families.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Lincoln County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Star City holds deed books from 1871

source_profiles.source_name: Lincoln County Deed Books -- County Clerk / Arkansas History Commission
source_profiles.repository: Lincoln County Clerk, Star City / Arkansas History Commission, Little Rock
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books for Lincoln County recording land conveyances, mortgages, and property transfers; County Clerk's office is the primary repository; Arkansas History Commission holds microfilm copies
source_profiles.survival_notes: Deed books complete from 1871; housed at County Clerk's office in Star City

source_profiles.source_name: Lincoln County Federal Census Records
source_profiles.repository: NARA / FamilySearch / Ancestry
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census records for Lincoln County; slave schedules (1850, 1860) document enslaved persons on delta plantations

source_profiles.source_name: Lincoln County Probate Records -- County Clerk / Arkansas History Commission
source_profiles.repository: Lincoln County Clerk, Star City / Arkansas History Commission, Little Rock
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, inventories, administrations, and estate records for Lincoln County; estate inventories frequently list enslaved persons by name, age, and value -- essential for African American genealogy

source_profiles.source_name: Lincoln County Marriage Records -- County Clerk / FamilySearch
source_profiles.repository: Lincoln County Clerk, Star City / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records for Lincoln County; includes both white and, after 1865, African American marriages; Freedmen's Bureau also recorded marriages for formerly enslaved couples

source_profiles.source_name: Lincoln County Federal Land Patents -- BLM / GLO
source_profiles.repository: Bureau of Land Management, General Land Office Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal land patents (original entries) for Lincoln County from the General Land Office; Arkansas is a public land state and most original land titles derive from federal patents; searchable by county and patentee name at BLM website

source_profiles.source_name: Lincoln County Freedmen's Bureau Records -- NARA / FamilySearch
source_profiles.repository: NARA / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Freedmen's Bureau records for Lincoln County including labor contracts, marriage records, school reports, and complaints; essential for tracing African American families in the transition from slavery to freedom

source_profiles.source_name: Lincoln County Cemetery Records -- Find A Grave / Arkansas Gravestone Project
source_profiles.repository: Find A Grave / Arkansas Gravestone Project / Local genealogical societies
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Cemetery transcriptions for Lincoln County churches and family burial grounds; Arkansas Gravestone Project and Find A Grave provide online access; local genealogical societies have published transcription volumes
